headlines.geopolitics: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check your AI detection confidence - most of us are overconfident about spotting deepfakes and generated content

clips.caption: When Duke accidentally agrees with Bree on AI regulation 😂
Dr. Pringle explains why we always think new technology will destroy us
Jax discovers the most Florida crime story of all time

script.text: Look, I'm not saying the government should regulate everything, but when a drug dealer is running a more efficient detention facility than the federal prison system, maybe we need to ask some questions.

Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th. Let's get you caught up. Starting with geopolitics — Iran's military is showing tactical adaptability that has U.S. officials concerned. They're specifically targeting what they see as American vulnerabilities, including air defense systems protecting our troops and assets in the region.
Well that's not ominous at all.

Moving to the economy — well, sort of. Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital are dealing with fallout from an external investigation. The report found administrators mishandled sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two affiliated doctors have already stepped down.
Institutional protection over victims. Again.

And domestically, a Fox News poll on artificial intelligence shows 87% of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only 47% find it useful right now. The gap between expectation and reality is, um, significant.
So we're all terrified of something we don't actually use. That tracks.
Eighty percent are concerned AI is eroding trust in news and social media, but 60% think they can spot AI-generated content. That confidence might be misplaced.
Overconfidence in the face of technological disruption. Tale as old as time.
Speaking of tales, Professor, we'll get your take in a bit. But first, let's dig into this Iran story because it represents something bigger about how adversaries adapt to American power.
So Bree, when you read that Iran is specifically targeting air defense systems — the things that protect our people — what's your immediate concern?
That we're playing defense when we should be asking why we have so many assets that need defending in the first place. I mean, how many bases do we actually need in the region?
Oh here we go. The problem isn't that Iran is targeting our defenses, it's that we have defenses to target?
The problem is that after twenty-five years of this, we still haven't figured out that military presence creates military targets.
And the alternative is what? We pack up and let Iran run the whole region?
The alternative is admitting that our current strategy isn't working. They're adapting to us faster than we're adapting to them.
That's actually what struck me about this reporting. U.S. officials are acknowledging Iranian tactical flexibility. That's not language we heard much during, say, the Iraq surge.
Because we learned that lesson the hard way. Look, I served there. You adapt or you get people killed. Iran's doing the same thing.
Right, so maybe— wait, did you just agree with me?
I agreed that adaptation matters. I didn't agree that we should abandon the region.
This is like watching my parents agree on the problem but fight about the solution. Which is basically every family dinner.
But here's what I want to understand, Duke. If Iran is successfully identifying and targeting our vulnerabilities, doesn't that suggest our defensive posture has predictable weaknesses?
Every defensive posture has weaknesses. The question is whether those weaknesses are acceptable given the alternatives.
But that's exactly my point. We're accepting casualties to maintain a presence that isn't actually containing Iranian influence.
Iranian influence over what? Because last I checked, they don't control Saudi Arabia, they don't control the UAE, they don't control Jordan.
They control significant proxy networks in Lebanon, Syria, Iraq, and Yemen. Our presence hasn't stopped that.
And without our presence, those proxy networks would be Iranian army bases.
Okay, so we have a fundamental disagreement about deterrence theory. Bree thinks our presence creates the problem. Duke thinks our absence would make it worse.
And I think Iran is just really good at playing the long game while we're over here playing checkers with our attention span.
That's actually not wrong. They've been consistent for decades. We change strategies every administration.
Sometimes consistency is just stubbornness with better marketing.
Professor, you've been listening to this back and forth. Where does history land on adversaries who successfully adapt to American military presence?
Well Leo, what we're witnessing is not unique to Iran or to this moment. Throughout history, smaller powers have survived by becoming students of larger powers' weaknesses.

The pattern is quite consistent. A dominant power establishes what it believes is an optimal defensive posture. The smaller power studies that posture obsessively, identifies the gaps, and develops tactics specifically to exploit them.
So like, Iran went to military school and we were the textbook?
In a sense, yes. Consider the Vietnamese against the French, then against us. Or the Afghan mujahideen against the Soviets. Or even the early Americans against the British.
Hold on, Professor. Are you saying the American Revolution was basically asymmetric warfare?
Duke, what was the Boston Tea Party if not a targeted attack on British economic vulnerabilities? What was the decision to avoid pitched battles if not adaptation to British military superiority?
Oh wow. So we literally wrote the playbook that everyone else is using against us now.
The irony is profound, Bree. The dominant power always believes its advantages are permanent. The challenging power always believes those advantages have expiration dates.
So what's the historical lesson for American policymakers? That our adversaries will always adapt, so we need to assume our current defensive posture is temporary?

The lesson is that adaptation is a race, not a destination. The British assumed their military superiority was sufficient. The French assumed the Maginot Line would hold. The Soviets assumed their Afghan strategy would work because it worked in Hungary.
In each case, the dominant power was fighting the last war while their opponents were preparing for the next one.
But Professor, doesn't that argue for maintaining our presence? If we leave, we stop learning how they're adapting to us.
Perhaps. Or perhaps it argues for questioning whether the framework itself is sound. Sometimes the wisest move is to change the game entirely.
Like when you realize you're playing chess and your opponent is playing poker?
That's surprisingly apt, Jax. Yes, sometimes the rules of engagement themselves become the trap.
Alright, let's shift gears. Jax, what caught your eye in the weird news this week?
Oh man, where do I start? First up, Palantir — you know, the company that makes AI weapons systems — has deployed their technology to help government workers find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?
Apparently the Pentagon's furniture procurement system was so broken they needed artificial intelligence to locate chairs. The same AI that tracks terrorists is now tracking office supplies.
That's the most government thing I've ever heard.
How much did this chair-finding AI cost taxpayers?
They won't say, which means it's either classified or embarrassing. Maybe both. But hey, at least the chairs are ergonomic now.
Speaking of government efficiency, Louisiana has until Friday to put the Ten Commandments in every classroom. Apparently Moses had strong opinions about homework policy.
The lawsuits on this are gonna be something to watch.
But my favorite story this week comes from Texas, where a candidate named 'Literally Anybody Else' is officially on the mayoral ballot.
That's honestly the most appealing candidate name I've heard in years.
Right? His platform is basically 'I am not the other guy.' Which, let's be honest, has won elections before.
Protest candidates have a long tradition in American politics. Though usually they choose more subtle names.
But the Florida story — oh my god, the Florida story. A drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months because the guy owed him money.
I'm sorry, months?
Months. The guy was living in a storage unit like it was a studio apartment. The drug dealer was bringing him food, checking on him, basically running a private prison.

Look, I'm not saying the government should regulate everything, but when a drug dealer is running a more efficient detention facility than the federal prison system, maybe we need to ask some questions.
Did the customer have a lease agreement? Was there a security deposit?
Bree, you're asking the real policy questions here. What's the regulatory framework for drug dealer imprisonment?
How did this even get discovered?
The storage facility manager got suspicious when someone kept visiting unit 47 with groceries. Turns out Florida Man was just trying to keep his debtor fed and healthy.
Debtors' prisons were outlawed in the 1830s, but apparently nobody told this fellow.
The fact that this happened in Florida is the least surprising part of this entire story.

Before we go, let's circle back to that AI poll because it connects to something important. Sixty percent of voters think they can spot AI-generated content, but research suggests most of us are overconfident about this.
So today's Daily Do is simple: test your AI detection skills. There are free tools online that show you examples of deepfakes, AI-generated images, and synthetic text. See how well you actually do.
I tried this last week and got maybe half right. It was humbling.
My daughter showed me some TikToks that I was absolutely convinced were real. They were completely AI-generated.
Wait, Duke's on TikTok now?
I'm not on TikTok. My daughter shows me TikToks. There's a difference.
The point is, if we're going to live in a world where AI can create convincing fake content, we need to get better at recognizing it. Overconfidence is dangerous.
As Benjamin Franklin once said, 'The doorstep to the temple of wisdom is a knowledge of our own ignorance.'
And as Florida Man apparently said, 'If you can't pay your debts, welcome to your new home in unit 47.' I'm Jax Mooney.
This has been The Friction. We'll see you tomorrow.
